content:
At LIDW 2026 in London, a panel debated whether Indian lawyers should be allowed to charge success fees.

One side said India is not ready. The other said it is already happening- quietly, informally- and the better question is whether to regulate it or keep pretending otherwise.

Both sides missed the more important point.

India has 52 million pending cases. After years of contentious litigation- multiple hearings, adjournments, appeals- the standard last order is: "No order as to costs."

Nobody pays anybody.

Which means there is no financial consequence for filing a case you know you will lose. 

No consequence for appealing an order you know will be upheld. No consequence for delaying enforcement for years. 

You take the chance. The other side pays to defend. The court bears the burden. And then you appeal anyway.

Success fees are a legitimate debate. But they are a distraction from this one.

The English "loser pays" system is not radical. It is the reason England's case pendency has historically been a fraction of India's. 

When losing costs you money- real money - you do not file frivolous cases. You do not delay. You do not appeal everything.

India has the legal framework to impose costs. Section 35 of the CPC allows it. 

Courts rarely use it. And when they do, the amounts are so nominal they change nothing.

The panel at London agreed on one thing- cost discipline works. It has been proven.

India has the law. India has the precedent. What it does not have is the judicial culture to use them.

Fix that. Then talk about success fees.

comment: Key bottleneck isn’t just awarding costs but execution and collection; nominal or uncollected cost orders blunt deterrence even when courts impose them.
